Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered throughout the course. I tried to dedicate about 20 hours a week, but it wasn't always at the same times. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lectures while doing other things around the house or commuting. I'd block out bigger chunks on weekends when I could focus better, and then squeeze in shorter sessions during weekday evenings when my extracurriculars allowed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I'm 24 and juggling quite a bit, so some weeks I was super consistent, and others I'd barely touch the course until a deadline loomed. When assignments were coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks I might only do the bare minimum to stay somewhat on track.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was checking if there were new announcements or seeing what assignments were coming up. Sometimes I'd get a reminder email or just feel guilty that I hadn't checked in for a few days. The discussion posts also pulled me in because I actually enjoyed connecting with other students and seeing different perspectives on the material.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. I'd check the LMS every morning with my coffee, almost like checking social media. It happened because I got into a rhythm with the discussion posts and wanted to keep up with the conversations. But honestly, that habit faded when things got busier with my other commitments.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could replay sections I didn't understand and really absorb the material that way. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing how other students interpreted things helped solidify my own understanding. I spent a lot of time on those videos—over two and a half hours worth—because they genuinely helped.
Q6: I tended to ignore a lot of the supplementary reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. Honestly, with 17 online courses worth of content, I had to prioritize what would actually impact my grade. The readings felt less engaging compared to listening to lectures, so they often got pushed aside unless they were directly tied to an assignment.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to assignments or exams, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that social element kept me motivated. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd probably skip it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required work had to get done no matter what, so that's where my energy went first. Optional stuff I'd only do if I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't often. There was less pressure but also less motivation to complete them.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and audio, then review them on my phone during commutes or while exercising. I also used my own notes extensively and would sometimes find YouTube videos that explained concepts differently. My actual study time was way more than what my login activity would suggest.
Q10: There were many times I'd be actively studying but the LMS wouldn't capture it at all. Like, I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while doing chores, but that only counted as one view. Or I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes and working through problems on paper. The system just couldn't see that kind of engagement.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet is reliable, but I preferred having materials saved so I could study anywhere without needing to be connected. This was especially true for the lecture recordings—I'd download them and listen repeatedly offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was really tough when multiple deadlines from different courses collided. My motivation dipped and even though my stress was manageable, I just felt drained. There were weeks where I'd log in, see the mountain of work, and feel overwhelmed enough that I'd only do the absolute minimum. My extracurriculars also competed for my attention during those periods.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused about the material, I'd use the LMS less effectively—just clicking through things without really absorbing them. Competing responsibilities from my other activities meant I sometimes had to choose between engaging deeply with the course or just getting something submitted. Workload definitely pushed me toward surface-level engagement at times.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. I'd be physically watching but mentally somewhere else, thinking about other stressors or just too tired to focus. The LMS would show I accessed the material, but I wasn't actually learning anything meaningful in those moments. It was frustrating because I knew I was wasting time.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Sometimes it was because I felt like I didn't have anything smart to contribute, or I was waiting until I understood the material better. Other times, I just procrastinated because the task felt overwhelming and I kept telling myself I'd do it tomorrow.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd have these bursts of intense engagement right before something was due, then quieter periods in between. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed with everything else on my plate. The deadlines created urgency that my natural motivation couldn't always provide.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, especially when it was encouraging or pointed out specific ways to improve. Good feedback made me want to engage more and try harder on the next assignment. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and feel less connected to the course. I appreciated when instructors acknowledged effort, not just results.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. I could work at my own pace, but that also meant I could fall behind easily. Having clear weekly modules helped me stay somewhat organized, but the sheer number of online courses I was taking made it hard to give each one the attention it deserved.
Q19: The video-based content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. But the low attendance requirements meant I didn't feel as accountable, which sometimes hurt my consistency. The discussion forums were well-designed and actually encouraged participation, which I appreciated. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or materials were poorly organized.
Q20: I think students might be searching for answers or trying to understand what's expected without actually doing the work yet. They could be overwhelmed and clicking around looking for clarity, or maybe they're engaged mentally but struggling to produce output. Sometimes high activity is just anxiety manifesting as checking and rechecking without making progress.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam scores weren't amazing, but I passed, and a lot of my real learning happened offline where the LMS couldn't track it. Students who learn effectively through downloaded materials or external resources might look disengaged online while actually doing fine. The LMS only captures one slice of the learning process.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access and timing, like when students are most active or how often they engage with specific resources. But they miss the quality and depth of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone was deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling, and they definitely can't capture offline study or emotional investment in learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas, and wanting to discuss concepts with others. For me, engagement also includes the emotional aspect—feeling like the course matters and that my effort is worthwhile. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually thought about course concepts outside of class, or the conversations I had with friends about the material. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to manage stress and stay focused, or how happy I generally felt despite the challenges. My resilience in pushing through difficult weeks despite low motivation—that's invisible in the data too.
